findings.title: Potential G704: SSRF via taint analysis
findings.description:
SSRF via taint analysis
Gosec confidence: 90%

attack_scenario.actor: An unauthenticated remote attacker who can control the targetURL parameter (e.g., via query string or form input).
attack_scenario.path: 1. Attacker sends a request to the vulnerable endpoint with targetURL set to an internal IP (e.g., http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/). 2. The handler at main.go:157 calls http.Get(targetURL) without validation. 3. The server makes an HTTP request to the attacker-specified URL, potentially accessing internal services.
attack_scenario.impact: The attacker can probe internal network services, read cloud metadata (e.g., AWS IAM credentials), or interact with internal APIs, leading to lateral movement and privilege escalation.
attack_scenario.explanation: The code directly uses user-supplied input in http.Get() without any allowlist or validation, enabling SSRF. This is a real vulnerability.

findings.title: Potential G701: SQL injection via taint analysis
findings.description:
SQL injection via taint analysis
Gosec confidence: 90%
findings.code_snippet:
71 | 70: 	query := "SELECT id, username, role FROM users WHERE username='" + username + "' AND password='" + password + "'"
71: 	row := db.QueryRow(query)
72:

attack_scenario.actor: An unauthenticated remote attacker who can supply username and password parameters (e.g., via login form).
attack_scenario.path: 1. Attacker submits a crafted username like ' OR '1'='1' -- and any password. 2. The query at main.go:70 becomes: SELECT id, username, role FROM users WHERE username='' OR '1'='1' --' AND password='...'. 3. The SQL injection bypasses authentication and returns all users.
attack_scenario.impact: Attacker gains unauthorized access to any user account, including admin, and can extract all user data from the database.
attack_scenario.explanation: String concatenation of user input into SQL query without parameterization is classic SQL injection. The finding is real.

findings.title: Potential G703: Path traversal via taint analysis
findings.description:
Path traversal via taint analysis
Gosec confidence: 90%

attack_scenario.actor: An unauthenticated remote attacker who can control the filename parameter (e.g., via query string).
attack_scenario.path: 1. Attacker sends a request with filename set to '../../etc/passwd'. 2. The path at main.go:135 becomes './files/../../etc/passwd' which resolves to '/etc/passwd'. 3. os.ReadFile at line 136 reads the file and returns its contents.
attack_scenario.impact: Attacker can read arbitrary files on the server, including configuration files, source code, and sensitive data (e.g., /etc/shadow, database credentials).
attack_scenario.explanation: User input is concatenated into a file path without sanitization, allowing directory traversal. The finding is real.

findings.title: Potential G702: Command injection via taint analysis
findings.description:
Command injection via taint analysis
Gosec confidence: 90%
[Also reported by another step: Subprocess launched with variable
Gosec confidence: 90%]

attack_scenario.actor: An unauthenticated remote attacker who can control the cmd parameter (e.g., via query string or form input).
attack_scenario.path: 1. Attacker sends a request with cmd set to 'valid_command; cat /etc/passwd'. 2. The handler at main.go:114 executes exec.Command('bash', '-c', cmd). 3. The shell interprets the injected command and executes it.
attack_scenario.impact: Attacker gains arbitrary command execution on the server, leading to full system compromise, data exfiltration, or lateral movement.
attack_scenario.explanation: User input is passed directly to a shell command via bash -c, enabling command injection. The finding is real.

findings.title: Potential G705: XSS via taint analysis
findings.description:
XSS via taint analysis
Gosec confidence: 90%

attack_scenario.actor: An unauthenticated remote attacker who can control the input parameter (e.g., via query string).
attack_scenario.path: 1. Attacker sends a request with input set to '<script>alert(1)</script>'. 2. The handler at main.go:210 writes JSON with the unsanitized input directly into the response body. 3. If the response is rendered in a browser (e.g., via JSONP or direct navigation), the script executes.
attack_scenario.impact: Attacker can execute arbitrary JavaScript in the victim's browser, leading to session hijacking, data theft, or phishing.
attack_scenario.explanation: User input is reflected in the response without encoding, and Content-Type is application/json, but browsers may still execute scripts if the response is loaded in a script context. This is a real XSS vulnerability.

findings.title: Potential G705: XSS via taint analysis
findings.description:
XSS via taint analysis
Gosec confidence: 90%
[AI Deep Verify: Attack scenario not viable — The response Content-Type is application/octet-stream, which forces binary download. No XSS vector exists because browsers will not render HTML/JavaScript from a binary stream.]

attack_scenario.explanation: The response Content-Type is application/octet-stream, which forces binary download. No XSS vector exists because browsers will not render HTML/JavaScript from a binary stream.

findings.title: Potential G705: XSS via taint analysis
findings.description:
XSS via taint analysis
Gosec confidence: 90%
[AI Deep Verify: Attack scenario not viable — The response Content-Type is text/plain, which browsers render as plain text, not HTML. No script execution is possible, so XSS is not feasible.]

attack_scenario.explanation: The response Content-Type is text/plain, which browsers render as plain text, not HTML. No script execution is possible, so XSS is not feasible.

findings.title: Potential G705: XSS via taint analysis
findings.description:
XSS via taint analysis
Gosec confidence: 90%

attack_scenario.actor: An unauthenticated remote attacker who can control the html parameter (e.g., via query string or form input).
attack_scenario.path: 1. Attacker sends a request with html set to '<script>alert(1)</script>'. 2. The handler at main.go:100 writes the raw HTML to the response with Content-Type defaulting to text/html. 3. The browser renders the script and executes it.
attack_scenario.impact: Attacker can execute arbitrary JavaScript in the victim's browser, leading to session hijacking, data theft, or phishing.
attack_scenario.explanation: User input is written directly to the response without escaping, and the Content-Type is likely text/html (default). This is a classic reflected XSS.

findings.title: Potential G401: Use of weak cryptographic primitive
findings.description:
Use of weak cryptographic primitive
Gosec confidence: 90%
findings.code_snippet:
206 | 205: 	// VULNERABLE: MD5 is cryptographically broken
206: 	hash := md5.Sum([]byte(input))
207: 	hexHash := fmt.Sprintf("%x", hash)

attack_scenario.actor: An attacker who can obtain MD5 hashes (e.g., via SQL injection or other data leaks).
attack_scenario.path: 1. Attacker extracts MD5 hashes from the database or intercepts them in transit. 2. Attacker uses rainbow tables or collision attacks to reverse the hash. 3. Attacker recovers the original input (e.g., password).
attack_scenario.impact: Attacker can recover plaintext passwords or other sensitive data from MD5 hashes, leading to account compromise.
attack_scenario.explanation: MD5 is cryptographically broken and vulnerable to collision and preimage attacks. Using it for hashing sensitive data is a real weakness.

findings.title: Potential G114: Use of net/http serve function that has no support for setting timeouts
findings.description:
Use of net/http serve function that has no support for setting timeouts
Gosec confidence: 90%

attack_scenario.actor: An attacker with network access to the server (e.g., on the same LAN or via SSRF).
attack_scenario.path: 1. Attacker opens many slow connections to the server. 2. Because no timeouts are set, connections remain open indefinitely. 3. Server resources are exhausted, leading to denial of service.
attack_scenario.impact: Denial of service: the server becomes unresponsive to legitimate requests, impacting availability.
attack_scenario.explanation: Using http.ListenAndServe without timeouts makes the server vulnerable to slowloris and other resource exhaustion attacks. This is a real but medium-severity issue.

findings.title: Potential G202: SQL string concatenation
findings.description:
SQL string concatenation
Gosec confidence: 90%
findings.code_snippet:
70 | 69: 	// VULNERABLE: string concatenation into SQL query
70: 	query := "SELECT id, username, role FROM users WHERE username='" + username + "' AND password='" + password + "'"
71: 	row := db.QueryRow(query)

attack_scenario.explanation: Duplicate of IRON-GOSEC-002, but still real.

findings.title: Potential Generic API key in config
findings.description: Regex pattern detected a potential Generic API key in config at main.go:31. This may be a hardcoded secret that automated scanners missed.

attack_scenario.actor: Any attacker with access to the source code repository (e.g., via a public repo, insider, or CI/CD leak).
attack_scenario.path: 1. Attacker obtains the source code (e.g., clones public repo or accesses leaked code). 2. Attacker reads line 31 of main.go and extracts the string 'sk-abc123def456ghi789jkl012mno345pqr678stu'. 3. Attacker uses this API key to authenticate to the corresponding service (e.g., OpenAI, Stripe) and make unauthorized API calls.
attack_scenario.impact: Attacker can make API calls on behalf of the application, potentially incurring financial charges, accessing sensitive data, or performing actions with the privileges associated with that key.
attack_scenario.explanation: The finding shows a hardcoded API key in source code. This is a real security issue because the key is directly embedded and can be extracted by anyone with code access. The key pattern 'sk-...' is typical for secret keys (e.g., OpenAI). No false positive conditions apply.

findings.title: SBOM generated: 1 components detected
findings.description: Software Bill of Materials (SBOM) generated in CycloneDX format. 1 components inventoried for vulnerability tracking.

findings.title: Not a git repository — supply chain checks limited
findings.description: Supply chain security checks (SBOM provenance, SLSA) require a git repository. Without git history, we cannot verify commit signatures or build provenance.

findings.title: SBOM generation available via syft
findings.description: Run 'syft scan <target> -o cyclonedx-json' to generate a Software Bill of Materials. SBOMs are required for supply chain transparency (US Executive Order 14028).

findings.title: OpenSSF Scorecard not installed
findings.description: Install OpenSSF Scorecard (https://github.com/ossf/scorecard) to get automated security health checks for your dependencies and build pipeline.
